expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: Sections 1–16 plus Appendix A are all present and substantively filled. §15 Decision Coverage Checklist contains all 22 rows: A1–A14 and B1–B8, each with a status and a non-trivial note. Example: "A3 Concurrency & consistency | Resolved | D2, §6 — UNIQUE (user_id, week_start_date) handles duplicate jobs; no application lock needed."

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions is a markdown table with columns: #, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation?. §12 Accepted Compromises is a table with columns: #, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger. Each compromise names the principle, e.g. C1: "Design for failure — ideal system would retry across the week" and a concrete trigger: "If blueprint changes to allow late delivery."

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D2 states: "A digest_sends table with a UNIQUE (user_id, week_start_date) constraint serves as the at-most-one-per-week guarantee. Before doing any work, DigestEmailJob attempts an INSERT; if the row already exists (duplicate job, scheduler re-run), it exits immediately without sending." The alternatives explicitly reject "Application-level check-before-insert: Race-prone (TOCTOU). Rejected."

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: D3 states: "the retry window is capped to the same calendar day (Monday). If all retries are exhausted, the digest_sends row is marked status='failed' and the send is dropped for that week. No attempt is made to deliver late." D3's Why section explicitly quotes the blueprint: "The blueprint explicitly states: 'a digest that arrives late is worse than one skipped.' This is a hard product requirement."

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D7 states: "The DigestSchedulerJob cron runs hourly at :00 UTC. Each run queries for users where ((8:00 AM in user.timezone) BETWEEN now() AND now() + 1 hour)." §7 "Late job start" states: "week_start_date is computed from the user's local time at job enqueue time, not job execution time. The content query uses Monday 00:00 local time as the window end, derived from the same week_start_date. A late start shifts send time but does not change the seven-day content window."

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: §7 states for no-activity: "Job exits before send if DigestContentQuery returns zero activity rows; digest_sends.status = 'skipped_no_activity'." For the mid-week new-user case, §7 "New user mid-week" states: "DigestContentQuery uses MAX(user.created_at, week_start_date_utc) as the lower bound of the activity window. This naturally covers only the days since the user joined." This explicitly clamps the window start to the user's creation date.

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D4 states: "Unsubscribe links embed an HMAC-SHA256 token derived from user_id + issued_week signed with a server-side secret." D5 states: "DigestContentQuery applies the same row-level permission filters used elsewhere in the application when fetching activity data. Items in a deleted workspace are excluded by joining against active workspaces. The query is never a raw 'all activity for this user_id.'"

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: D1 and D7 together name the scheduling trade-off (single fixed UTC cron vs. hourly computed timezone filter vs. precomputed next_send_at column) and resolve with principle-grounded reasoning citing Simplicity and Principle of least surprise. §6 Bounded operations explicitly states: "Highlighted items are capped at ten in the query (LIMIT 10), matching the blueprint's stated cap. The '+N more' count is derived from a separate COUNT(*) query with the same filters but without the LIMIT, or from a LIMIT 11 query where an 11th result signals overflow."
